figures.gloss: The rasped root-bark of Tabernanthe iboga, eaten in small doses to sustain the all-night liturgy and, once in a life, in a massive initiatory dose that lays the novice out for days — sent across the boundary of death to see Bwiti and the ancestors with their own eyes.
figures.facet: The tradition's entire epistemology in one plant: Bwiti asks not for belief but for eyewitness — banzie contrast the mission's God, merely preached, with the God eboga lets them see — and the initiatory dose is deliberately calibrated to the edge of death.
figures.provenance: James W. Fernandez, Bwiti: An Ethnography of the Religious Imagination in Africa (1982); the ritual use long predates the plant's botanical description (Baillon, 1889).

figures.gloss: The recurring worship of Bwiti: from dusk to dawn the banzie dance the chapel's cycle of creation, death, and return around its carved central pillar — the road between the worlds — sustained by small doses of eboga and steered by the ngombi harp.
figures.facet: Liturgy as endurance: the night-long circuit does with community, song, and fatigue what the great dose does with chemistry — a weekly, survivable rehearsal of the one-time crossing.
figures.provenance: Fernandez (1982), whose Fang fieldwork (1958-60) centers on the ngozé; Świderski's Gabon studies.

figures.gloss: The great female principle of Fang Bwiti theology — the Sister of God: source of fecundity, blood, and hidden knowledge, honoured as the compassionate center of a cosmology whose male creator stands remote.
figures.facet: The affective center of the cult: where the creator recedes, the Sister remains — fecundity and esoteric knowledge gathered into a feminine mediator through whom the distant godhead is approached.
figures.provenance: Fernandez (1982) on Fang Bwiti cosmology; her prominence varies by branch.

figures.gloss: Bwiti's initiates — the community of men and women who have eaten the great dose of eboga and crossed over once, and who therefore worship as witnesses rather than believers.
figures.facet: Initiation as citizenship: banzie status cuts across clan and, in the syncretic branches, across mission denominations — a new kinship made of shared vision rather than shared blood.
figures.provenance: Fernandez (1982), who renders the initiates' self-designation banzie as 'angels'.

figures.gloss: The eight-stringed cult harp, its neck often carved as a woman's head, whose music leads the ngozé and, at initiation, reaches the novice on the far side — played as the voice of Nyingwan Mebege calling the traveler home.
figures.facet: A sonic lifeline: the harp is less an accompaniment than a psychopomp in wood and string — the one thread the disembodied initiate can follow back to the living.
figures.provenance: Fernandez (1982); the harp's identification with the Sister of God is standard in accounts of Fang Bwiti liturgy (also Świderski).

edges.note: One sacrament, two dosages: the ngozé runs on the small eating that keeps the dancers awake; initiation on the great eating that sends the novice across.
The harp is her instrument and her voice — the Sister of God made audible in the chapel, night after night.
GOLD SEAM: the same sacrament — the aggregate entheogenic shard's Gabon panel, now promoted into Bwiti's own shard; one cult described from two directions.
The Nima of the aggregate shard plays this very harp — initiator and instrument belong to the same Bwiti liturgy, split across two shards and here re-seamed.

GOLD SEAM: Fang Bwiti's syncretic layer is attested history — mission Christianity (1900s-40s) folded into the liturgy, God and Christ absorbed under Fang names without displacing eboga; the missions fought it, the chapels kept both.

Both are eaten sacraments through which the divine becomes present in the communicant — but Bwiti stakes everything on direct vision where the eucharist works by faith; the banzie themselves draw the contrast.
In Christianized chapels the Sister of God is read alongside the Virgin — a feminine mediator before a remote godhead; the equation is reported unevenly across branches, so the seam stays faint.

dossier.essence: Bwiti is an initiatory religion of Gabon, born among the Mitsogo and Apindji peoples and reworked around 1890-1920 by the Fang into syncretic chapel communities that fold mission Christianity into an ancestral liturgy. Its center is the eating of eboga (the root-bark of Tabernanthe iboga): a massive once-in-a-lifetime dose sends the initiate across the boundary of death to see the ancestors and Bwiti directly.
dossier.cosmos: In Fang Bwiti theology a remote creator stands behind the world, approached through Nyingwan Mebege, the Sister of God — source of fecundity, blood, and hidden knowledge; the dead remain reachable along the road the initiate travels under eboga. Death is a crossing the initiated have already rehearsed: the great dose is understood as dying in this life and returning with eyewitness knowledge of the beyond.
dossier.practice: Worship is the ngozé, an all-night liturgy from dusk to dawn in which initiates (banzie, 'angels') dance the cycle of creation, death, and return around the chapel's carved central pillar, sustained by small doses of eboga and led by the eight-stringed ngombi harp. Initiation requires the great dose, taken once under supervision at the calibrated edge of physical danger; the community it creates cuts across clan lines, and the religion has grown from an ethnic cult toward a Gabonese national institution.
dossier.canon: No scripture — an oral and liturgical corpus of origin myths, songs, and harp music; the standard scholarly record is Fernandez's ethnography of Fang Bwiti (fieldwork 1958-60), with the Mitsogo branch documented by later French-language anthropology.
dossier.reflects: The demand for eyewitness over belief — a religion that stakes its entire authority on direct, chemically attained vision of the dead rather than on preaching or faith.
sources: James W. Fernandez, 'Bwiti: An Ethnography of the Religious Imagination in Africa' (1982)
André Mary, 'Le défi du syncrétisme: le travail symbolique de la religion d'Eboga (Gabon)' (1999)
Julien Bonhomme, 'Le miroir et le crâne: parcours initiatique du Bwete Misoko (Gabon)' (2006)

links.cite: James W. Fernandez, 'Bwiti: An Ethnography of the Religious Imagination in Africa' (1982)
Julien Bonhomme, 'Le miroir et le crâne: parcours initiatique du Bwete Misoko (Gabon)' (2006)
